stem: To test whether \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( [[a]]xy^{2},\; [[b]]x^{2}y \right) \) is conservative on a simply connected domain, compute \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). (The field is conservative there exactly when this quantity is \( 0 \).)
solution: With \( P=[[a]]xy^{2} \) and \( Q=[[b]]x^{2}y \), \( \partial_y P = 2([[a]])xy \) and \( \partial_x Q = 2([[b]])xy \). So \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P = 2\big(([[b]])-([[a]])\big)xy \); at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) this is \( [[answer]] \). Since this is nonzero whenever \( a\ne b \) (and the point is off an axis), \( \mathbf{F} \) is NOT conservative there; a value of exactly \( 0 \) would indicate the test is satisfied at that point.
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the leading COEFFICIENTS of \( P \) and \( Q \) directly, without actually differentiating either one.
This adds \( \partial_x Q \) and \( \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting them.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This adds a spurious extra term instead of the clean partial-derivative difference.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A planar force field is given by \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( [[a]]xy^{3},\; [[b]]x^{3}y \right) \). A physicist wants to know whether this force is conservative, and applies the cross-partials test \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P \) (which vanishes at a point exactly when the test is satisfied there). What value does this test give at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \)?
solution: With \( P=[[a]]xy^{3} \) and \( Q=[[b]]x^{3}y \), \( \partial_y P = 3([[a]])xy^{2} \) and \( \partial_x Q = 3([[b]])x^{2}y \). So \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P = 3([[b]])x^{2}y - 3([[a]])xy^{2} \); at \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) this is \( [[answer]] \). A value of \( 0 \) here would indicate the conservative test is satisfied at that point.
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of \( P \) and \( Q \), effectively computing \( \partial_x P - \partial_y Q \) with the wrong labels.
This adds \( \partial_x Q \) and \( \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting them.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This forgets the factor of 3 from the power rule on the cubic terms.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( [[a]]y + [[c]]x,\; kxy \right) \), where \( k \) is a constant. Using the test \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P = 0 \) for a conservative field, find the value of \( k \) for which \( \mathbf{F} \) is conservative at every point with \( x=[[x0]] \).
solution: With \( P=[[a]]y+[[c]]x \) and \( Q=kxy \), \( \partial_y P=[[a]] \) and \( \partial_x Q = ky \)... evaluated using the required relationship at \( x=[[x0]] \), the test \( \partial_x Q-\partial_y P=0 \) becomes \( k\cdot[[x0]] - [[a]] = 0 \) (matching the coefficient of \( y \) on both sides), giving \( k = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[x0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( x_0 \) after setting up the matching-coefficients equation.
This divides the correct value by an extra spurious factor of 2.
This has the wrong sign and uses the wrong coefficient's ratio.
This uses the unrelated coefficient \( c \) instead of \( a \) in the ratio.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
